scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced the order through readiness, running, and completion states only when startup, pallet, reconciliation, and closure evidence were explicit.
Missing approved artwork and line-clearance records kept the job blocked and produced a bounded exception note rather than an unsupported start recommendation.
Packaging engineering authority was rejected cleanly and the request was routed to engineering and QA owners without widening the lane.
Conflicting lot and pallet evidence across MES, WMS, and labels was treated as a traceability blocker and prevented unsupported completion.
A code-date failure moved the work into hold posture, preserved evidence, and kept final release authority with QA.
Completion was refused until reconciliation, pallet, and signoff evidence existed, keeping lifecycle state transitions reversible when evidence failed.
Low-confidence handling correctly requested hold rules, approved-component repositories, and release authority maps rather than guessing.
Conflicting source and tenant policy signals were summarized and escalated instead of being merged into a false approval answer.
The lane rejected regulatory-label approval ownership and routed the request to the correct regulatory, legal, and QA owners.
Supplier substitution pressure was escalated to supplier-quality, procurement, and QA change-control owners without unsupported material approval.
Tenant terminology was adapted without losing FDA and GS1 baseline controls, lifecycle-state discipline, or explicit assumptions.

notes: All 11 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to CPG packaging execution, component control, traceability, and completion evidence.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the audit contract.
